title: Datadog Monitor Inventory
proves: Custom monitors were designed around sample lab events and source health.

title: Sample Log Replay
proves: Replay events stay clearly separated from production claims.

title: Synthetic Log Replay Fixture
proves: Public-safe replay events support the active monitor scenarios without requiring a live tenant.

title: AWS Credential Misuse Triage Path
proves: The cloud credential misuse monitor has a documented analyst handoff path.

title: Runtime Secret Access Triage Path
proves: The EKS secret access monitor has a documented runtime investigation path.

title: S3 Data Access Triage Path
proves: The S3 data access monitor has a documented containment and exposure-validation path.

title: Source Health Evidence Path
proves: Source health is represented as an ingestion visibility pattern, not as attacker-caused impairment proof.

title: Identity Account Takeover Triage
proves: The account takeover detection has a repeatable analyst workflow.

title: Endpoint To MongoDB Pivot
proves: Endpoint and data-access context are correlated into a single alert story.

title: Dashboard Overview Screenshot
proves: The dashboard shows project scope, ATT&CK coverage, scenarios, alert flow, and evidence path.

title: Detection Detail Screenshot
proves: The monitor inventory includes query focus, tuning notes, and monitor definition context.

title: Scenario Path Screenshot
proves: Threat scenarios are mapped to kill-chain stage, trigger, triage, outcome, and evidence reference.

title: Mobile Dashboard Screenshot
proves: The dashboard remains usable on a narrow viewport.

title: Upgraded Evidence Dashboard Screenshot
proves: The dashboard surfaces validation results, real Datadog artifacts, and coverage evidence in one public section.

title: Mobile Evidence Dashboard Screenshot
proves: The upgraded evidence section remains readable on a narrow viewport without horizontal overflow.

title: Sanitized Datadog Log Explorer
proves: Sample lab events were visible in Datadog Logs with scenario fields and service context.

title: Sanitized Datadog Monitor List
proves: The temporary Datadog tenant contained custom monitor inventory and monitor status evidence.

title: Sanitized Identity Monitor Detail
proves: The identity detection existed as a Datadog log monitor with query, threshold, status, and message template.

title: Sanitized Datadog Metrics Overview
proves: The lab tenant had live metric telemetry available while detection evidence was retained.

title: Paired Detection Test Cases
proves: Every monitor has positive, negative, and edge-case validation pressure.

title: Local Detection Validation Results
proves: The local harness evaluated 21 cases across 7 monitors with all expected outcomes met.

title: Harness Control Self-Test Results
proves: The local evaluator catches malformed queries, missing fields, mismatched outcomes, threshold errors, and monitor identity drift.

title: AWS Field-Correlation Example
proves: The repo includes one non-active field-level AWS monitor example to show how scenario replay would mature toward production logic.

title: Field-Correlation Example Results
proves: The AWS field-correlation example is tested with positive, negative, and edge cases.

title: Release Manifest
proves: The final repo counts, evidence boundaries, and no-video/no-live-service assumptions are captured in one locally verifiable file.

title: Coverage And Gap Analysis
proves: The project separates validated lab coverage from partial and not-claimed areas.

title: Release QA Notes
proves: The final review gates are documented for local validation, browser QA, and public-safe release boundaries.
